First, the problem says that the input string can be up to 1e5 characters long. So a brute-force approach where I check every possible substring of length 10 and compare it with all others would be O(n^2), which is definitely not feasible for n=1e5. That would be way too slow.

Hmm, so what's a better way? Maybe using a hash table to keep track of all the 10-letter sequences and their counts. That way, I can iterate through the string once, add each 10-length substring to the hash table, and then finally collect those that have a count greater than 1. That sounds manageable. The time complexity here would be O(n) for building the hash map and O(n) for checking the counts, which is acceptable. But space could be an issue. How many unique 10-length sequences can there be? Well, since each character is one of four, there are 4^10 possibilities. 4^10 is 1,048,576 which is around a million, which is manageable for a hash table. So that's feasible.

But wait, even for a string of length 1e5, there are (n-9) possible substrings of length 10. For n=1e5, that's about 99991 entries. So the hash table approach is viable.

1. Iterate through the string from index 0 to len(s) - 10.
2. For each starting index, take the substring of length 10.
3. Use a hash map (like unordered_map in C++) to count occurrences of each substring.
4. After processing all substrings, collect those with count > 1.

Each character is A, C, G, T. Each can be represented as 2 bits. For a 10-letter sequence, that's 20 bits. So we can encode each substring into an integer. That would reduce the space and possibly speed up the hashing.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> findRepeatedDnaSequences(string s) {
        if (s.length() < 10) return {};
        unordered_map<int, int> counts;
        vector<string> result;
        // precompute 4^9
        const int four_power_nine = 262144;
        // compute initial val
        int val = 0;
        for (int j = 0; j < 10; ++j) {
            val = (val << 2) | char_to_num(s[j]);
        }
        // process first val
        // then process the rest
        for (int i = 1; i <= s.length() - 10; ++i) {
            int old_char_val = char_to_num(s[i-1]);
            int new_char_val = char_to_num(s[i + 9]);
            val = ((val - (old_char_val * four_power_nine)) * 4 + new_char_val);
            // check counts and add to result
            if (counts[val] == 1) {
                result.push_back(s.substr(i, 10));
            }
            counts[val]++;
        }

So the code outline is:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> findRepeatedDnaSequences(string s) {
        if (s.length() < 10) return {};
        unordered_map<int, int> counts;
        vector<string> result;
        // precompute 4^9
        const int four_power_nine = 262144;
        // compute initial val
        int val = 0;
        for (int j = 0; j < 10; ++j) {
            val = (val << 2) | char_to_num(s[j]);
        }
        // process first val
        // then process the rest
        for (int i = 1; i <= s.length() - 10; ++i) {
            int old_char_val = char_to_num(s[i-1]);
            int new_char_val = char_to_num(s[i + 9]);
            val = ((val - (old_char_val * four_power_nine)) * 4 + new_char_val);
            // check counts and add to result
            if (counts[val] == 1) {
                result.push_back(s.substr(i, 10));
            }
            counts[val]++;
        }
        // but wait, the initial val is not processed yet. The first val is for i=0. Need to process it.

        // Also, the code above starts processing from i=1, but the first val (for i=0) needs to be checked.

        // So the code should first process the initial val.

        // So:

        counts[val] = 1;

        for (int i = 1; i <= s.length() - 10; ++i) {
            ... compute new val ...
            if (counts.find(val) != counts.end()) {
                if (counts[val] == 1) {
                    result.push_back(s.substr(i, 10));
                }
            } else {
                counts[val] = 1;
            }
            // Or better, use the same approach as before.
